one_line_takeaway: Evaluating machine unlearning on randomly chosen forget sets is optimistic; a bi-level-optimization search for the WORST-case forget set is both efficient (via signSGD unrolling) and reveals that popular relabeling-based unlearning methods give a false sense of forgetting, while worst-case points turn out to be exactly the non-coreset, spuriously-correlated data.
key_finding: The worst-case forget set is both real and diagnostic. (1) Even exact unlearning (Retrain) is genuinely challenged: on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 its UA drops from ~5-6% (random) to 0.00% and MIA from ~13-14% to ~0% across 1/5/10/20% forgetting ratios, with much lower variance and NO utility loss (TA even slightly higher, connecting to coresets). (2) The audit cleanly separates method families: relabeling-FREE methods (FT, EU-k, CF-k, SCRUB, l1-sparse) track Retrain's failure under worst-case sets (their avg gap to Retrain shrinks, e.g. SCRUB 9.91->0.82, CF-k 4.36->0.08), whereas relabeling-BASED methods (RL, BE, BS, SalUn) give a 'false sense of unlearning' — their MIA gap to Retrain explodes under worst-case sets (RL 96.70, SalUn 96.43, BE 81.45, BS 74.88), inflating avg gaps to 24.5-37.2. (3) Interpretability: the COMPLEMENT of the worst-case forget set behaves like a coreset — training only on it matches EL2N/GraNd and on CIFAR-100 even exceeds full-data TA at 90%/95% keep ratios — so worst-case points are non-coreset, spuriously-correlated items (on biased CelebA, 45.9% are Blond+Female). (4) The failure generalizes to text-to-image: an SD model unlearned (via ESD) on worst-case prompts still generates the 'forgotten' concept, unlike random-prompt unlearning.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the notion of the 'worst-case forget set' for evaluating machine unlearning (MU), arguing that the standard practice of evaluating MU on RANDOMLY chosen forget sets under-reports how badly unlearning can fail. Its own contribution is (1) formulating worst-case forget-set identification as a bi-level optimization (BLO) problem where the upper level selects a forget set (a data-selection mask w) to MAXIMIZE the difficulty of influence erasure while the lower level performs unlearning; (2) a scalable first-order solver that uses sign-based gradient unrolling (signSGD as the lower-level optimizer), which the authors show drives the implicit gradient term to zero almost surely, collapsing the BLO to alternating PGD (upper) and signSGD (lower); and (3) an empirical audit showing this worst-case lens exposes failures — especially a 'false sense of unlearning' in relabeling-based methods — and a coreset-selection interpretation of the identified sets. The authors claim to be the first to identify worst-case forget sets for MU. Note: the 9 unlearning methods audited (Retrain, FT, EU-k, CF-k, SCRUB, l1-sparse, RL, BE, BS, SalUn) and coreset baselines (EL2N, GraNd) are prior work, not this paper's methods — the paper's own artifact is the worst-case-forget-set SELECTOR and evaluation protocol layered on top of them.
threat_model: This is an adversarial EVALUATION / robustness-audit framework, not an attack on a deployed system. Machine unlearning itself is a privacy/security-relevant primitive (right-to-be-forgotten compliance, defending against membership inference, removing poisoned or copyrighted data). The paper adopts an adversarial viewpoint in the sense of a worst-case data-selection 'adversary' who picks the subset of training points hardest to erase, so as to stress-test an unlearning method rather than to accept optimistic average-case (random forget set) numbers. It is explicitly positioned as complementary to — not a replacement for — existing adversarial metrics like membership inference attacks (MIA) and adversarial prompts. There is no external attacker exfiltrating data; the 'worst case' is an auditing construct. The security payoff is reliability: it reveals that some approximate unlearning methods only appear to forget under favorable (random) evaluation.
limitations: Scope is vision (image classification primarily, plus a text-to-image extension); no LLM/graph/federated evaluation despite those being motivated. The worst-case selector is itself tied to an FT-style lower-level unlearning objective (lambda=1, forget loss = -retain loss), so the identified 'worst case' may be partly specific to that surrogate rather than universally worst across all unlearning objectives. The BLO relaxation (continuous w, top-m rounding) and signSGD implicit-gradient=0 approximation trade rigor for scalability — the paper does not bound the resulting selection sub-optimality. Forget-set size m is fixed a priori. The provocative claim that inability of Retrain to reduce worst-case UA below the random baseline should make us 'rethink' exact unlearning is raised only in discussion, not resolved. Prompt-wise results are qualitative (image examples), with quantitative UnlearnCanvas numbers not surfaced in the extracted pages. Compute cost of the bi-level search vs. plain random evaluation is not tabulated.

datasets_used: CelebA (Blond vs Non-Blond hair, gender spurious-correlation case study)
UnlearnCanvas (prompt-wise text-to-image unlearning benchmark; 10 objects x 10 styles = 100 prompt combinations, 10% unlearned)
quantitative_results: Retrain on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: UA drops from random ~5.28-5.92% to worst-case 0.00% across 1/5/10/20% forgetting ratios (Diff 5.28-5.92 lower)
Retrain MIA drops from random ~12.86-14.34% to worst-case ~0.00-0.03% (Diff up to 14.31 lower); RA stays ~100%, TA slightly HIGHER under worst-case (e.g. +0.56 at 20%)
At 10% forgetting, relabeling-free avg-gap-to-Retrain shrinks under worst-case: SCRUB 9.91->0.82, CF-k 4.36->0.08, EU-k 4.04->3.28, FT 2.00->1.37, l1-sparse 2.31->1.72
Relabeling-based methods worsen drastically under worst-case: avg gap RL 4.38->24.88, BE 4.28->34.70, BS 6.84->37.22, SalUn 1.89->24.51
Worst-case MIA for relabeling-based methods spikes far above Retrain's 0%: RL 96.70%, SalUn 96.43%, BE 81.45%, BS 74.88% (vs Retrain 0.00%)
BE/BS worst-case UA rises to 19.47%/29.75% (diverging from Retrain's 0%), indicating failure to forget
Complement of worst-case forget set trained as coreset matches EL2N/GraNd on CIFAR-10/100; on CIFAR-100 exceeds full-dataset TA at 90% and 95% keep ratios
CelebA worst-case forget set composition: 45.9% Blond+Female, 25.0% Blond+Male, 24.7% Non-Blond Male, 4.5% Non-Blond Female

Appendix Table A1 confirms the relabeling-based failure holds across ALL forgetting ratios (1/5/10/20%): worst-case MIA gap to Retrain for RL rises 21.36->24.08->24.88->25.74 (avg gap) with MIA up to ~97.5% at 20%; SalUn avg gap 18.41->23.44->24.51->25.29; BS/BE avg gaps reach 43-45 / 34-42; relabeling-free methods (CF-k avg gap ~0.08-0.24, FT/l1-sparse ~1.4-4) stay close to Retrain throughout

Solver settings: upper-level PGD 20 iterations (converges), lower-level signSGD K=10 epochs, gamma=1e-4, alpha=1e-3; results over 10 trials. Prompt-wise: ESD+signSGD lr 1e-5 for 1000 iters to select worst-case prompts, then ESD lr 3e-7 for 1000 iters to unlearn; DDIM 100 steps, guidance 7.5
baselines_compared: EU-k (exact unlearning of last k layers)
CF-k (catastrophic forgetting of last k layers)
Coreset baselines EL2N and GraNd, plus random selection, for the coreset analysis
Random forget set (the average-case baseline the paper argues against)
